name: Chico Freeman
abstract: Chico Freeman (born Earl Lavon Freeman Jr.; July 17, 1949) is a modern jazz tenor saxophonist and trumpeter and son of jazz saxophonist Von Freeman. He began recording as lead musician in 1976 with Morning Prayer, won the New York Jazz Award in 1979 and earned the Stereo Review Record of the Year in 1981 for his album The Outside Within.

interviews.title: Chico Freeman interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 13, 2001

works.t: What Ever Happened To The Dream Deferred

works_created.t: What Ever Happened To The Dream Deferred

quotes.text: You know Charlie Parker was in that band, Dizzy Gillespie, Hubert Laws. So yeah, I was with Machito for I don’t know four or five years. And so I’ve played a lot of Afro-Cuban music. I’ve been to Cuba and I’ve played with Irakere and Chucho Valdez and some of the great musicians down there. I got to hear and learn about Chano Pozo and — you know I’d never met him but a guy named Changuito, who was one of the legends in Cuba at the conguero, and as well here with Tito Puente, I played with Tito Puente and Eddie Palmieri. I did a concert at Carnegie Hall where they brought McCoy Tyner and Eddie Palmieri together and I played with that situation, it was great. So yeah I’ve done a lot of Latin music, I love it, Ray Barretto, and I enjoy it very much.
quotes.caption: Chico Freeman recalls a Latin jazz concert at Carnegie Hall
quotes.doc_title: Chico Freeman interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 13, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Jack DeJohnette
about_quotes.evidence: when David and Black Arthur Blythe leave then Chico Freeman and John Purcell come in... For a while we had Chico, Purcell and Murray I believe.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jack DeJohnette — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Mcbee
about_quotes.evidence: I performed at the Carnegie Hall in 1984 with Chico Freeman, just a duet. Yeah, I remember that.

about_quotes.speaker: John Malachi
about_quotes.evidence: I never did know what his first was -- we used to call him 'Bruz' and his last name was Freeman... [INTERVIEWER] Chico. [INTERVIEWEE] Yeah.

about_quotes.speaker: Charles Mingus
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] Yeah, sure. Have you heard Chico Freeman? ... [INTERVIEWEE] A player should be an actor, a great actor.

about_quotes.speaker: Sudhalter
about_quotes.evidence: She has played flute and saxophone with Latin Fever, Etta Jones and Chico Freeman

about_quotes.speaker: Kenny Barron
about_quotes.evidence: Chico Freeman at Ronnie Scott’s. Yeah, I’ve seen that, not in years.
about_quotes.doc_title: Kenny Barron — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
